envelopes.hydrated_text:
Looking for a sanity check on a Roth conversion strategy. Recently retired at 60, low-six-figures Trad IRA balance, no current taxable income outside Social Security I haven't yet claimed. Plan is to convert to fill the 12% bracket each year through age 70, then claim SS and start RMDs at 73 with a smaller Trad IRA balance.

The questions where I'd appreciate community input: (1) Am I missing IRMAA Medicare surcharge thresholds in my conversion sizing? Looking at the 2026 thresholds, filling to the top of the 12% bracket appears to stay under the first IRMAA tier, but I want a second pair of eyes. (2) Is there a tax-loss-harvesting interaction I should be planning around? I have some tax-loss carryforwards from 2022 still on file. (3) State tax — I'm in a state that taxes Roth conversions at ordinary rates. Does anyone have a worked example of how to incorporate state tax into the bracket-filling decision?

Thanks in advance. The community has been helpful on prior similar questions.

Roth conversions executed in early retirement remain a high-leverage tax-planning strategy for many households, but the introduction of cliff effects from IRMAA Medicare surcharges has complicated the optimisation. We walk through the 2026 IRMAA thresholds and discuss how to integrate them into a conversion-sizing model.

The IRMAA tiers are not gradual: crossing a threshold by even $1 triggers the higher surcharge for the entire two-year lookback window applicable to that year's premium. This makes thresholds operate as effective marginal-rate cliffs rather than smooth tax increases. For households where IRMAA thresholds bind, the optimal conversion is generally either to fill exactly to the threshold or to push substantially above it; there is rarely a case where landing just above a threshold is optimal.

We also discuss the interaction with NIIT (Net Investment Income Tax), which separately applies to investment income above its own threshold. Households where both IRMAA and NIIT thresholds bind face a piecewise-linear-with-cliffs effective tax schedule that requires careful modelling.

Roth conversion planning involves trading off current-year tax cost against expected future-year tax cost on the same dollars. The decision is straightforward in textbook cases (current rate definitively below future rate) and complex in realistic cases where future rates depend on portfolio growth, future tax law, claiming decisions for Social Security, and Medicare premium tiers.

This wiki page synthesises community experience on the major decision factors and provides links to several worked examples covering common situations: pre-Social-Security gap years, large IRA balances facing future RMD pressure, surviving-spouse tax-bracket consequences, and charitable-bequest planning that interacts with IRA balances.

The most common error we see in community questions is over-confidence in any single multi-decade projection. We recommend treating conversion sizing as a year-by-year decision under uncertainty, with an annual review of the previous year's assumptions against actuals.

bullets: Roth conversion sizing is high-leverage in early retirement but complicated by IRMAA cliff effects: crossing a threshold by even $1 triggers a two-year surcharge, so optimal conversions are typically right at a threshold or substantially above it, never just above. (env-14-a, env-14-b)

NIIT and IRMAA can simultaneously bind for higher-balance households, producing a piecewise-linear-with-cliffs effective tax schedule that requires careful modelling rather than rule-of-thumb bracket-filling. (env-14-b)

The most common error in community Roth-conversion questions is over-confidence in multi-decade projections; the recommended approach is year-by-year sizing with annual review against actuals. (env-14-c)
